DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-12-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Atty. BRYAN D. LAMB, ESQ. of Lamb & Frischer Law Firm LLP (Tel: 415-997-9529) appearing for plaintiffs Luu Tran and Hui Lu;
Atty. BARRY BRYAN, ESQ. of Bassi Edlin Huie & Blum (Tel: 415-397-9006) appearing for defendant Winspear Logistics Corporation and Malcolm Winspear;
Atty. CHALSIE KELLER, ESQ. of Siegel, Moreno & Stettler (Tel: 619-525-7626) appearing via CourtCall for plaintiff Applied Risk Services;
Atty. STEPHANIE B. GONZALEZ, ESQ. of Fitzpatrick & Hunt, Pagano, Aubert, LLP (Tel: 213-873-2139) appearing via CourtCall for defendant Swissport Cargo Services

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS WINSPEAR LOGISTICS CORPORATION AND MALCOLM WINSPEAR'S DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows, making one addition: Defendants Winspear Logistics Corporation and Malcolm Winspear's demurrer to complaint is sustained with leave to amend. Plaintiffs have 20 days leave to amend to allege alter ego liability.

The court previously overruled defendant Javelin Logistics Company, Inc.'s demurrer because plaintiff alleges facts regarding successor liability. (First Amended Complaint, Paras. 31-34 and 38; McClellan v. Northridge Park Townhome Owners Ass'n, Inc. (2001) 89 Cal.App.4th 746, 753 [successor corporation can be liable where "the transaction amounts to a consolidation or merger of the two corporations" or "the purchasing corporation is merely a continuation of the selling corporation."].)

The focus of this demurrer is whether plaintiffs' alter ego allegations are sufficient. Successor liability and alter ego liability are distinct equitable concepts. (See Brown Bark III, L.P. v. Haver (2013) 219 Cal.App.4th 809, 823 ["Although the showing required to invoke these two equitable doctrines is different, their effect is the same."].) Plaintiffs have leave to amend to allege ultimate facts showing that defendants Winspear Logistics and Malcom Winspear are alter egos of each other. (See Rutherford Holdings, LLC v. Plaza Del Rey
223 Cal.App.4th 221, 236 [plaintiff sufficiently pled alter ego theory of liability where it alleged that "Caswell dominated and controlled PDR; that a unity of interest and ownership existed between Caswell and PDR; that PDR was a mere shell and conduit for Caswell's affairs; that PDR was inadequately capitalized; that PDR failed to abide by the formalities of corporate existence; that Caswell used PDR assets as her own; and that recognizing the separate existence of PDR would promote injustice."].)

The demurrer is overruled as to the voidable transfer claim.

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano, CSR#8600, e-mail: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-05-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Atty. ANDREW C. JOHNSON, ESQ. of Condon & Forsyth, LLP (Tel: 410-557-2030) appearing via CourtCall for Menzies Aviation (USA) Inc.;
No other appearances made in this matter.

HEARING RE: CROSS-DEFENDANT MENZIES AVIATION (USA) INC.'S DEMURRER TO CROSS-COMPLAINT.

Matter submitted without argument. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Cross-defendant Menzies Aviation (USA) Inc.'s demurrer to the amended cross-complaint of the City and County of San Francisco and San Francisco International Airport based upon Code of Civil Procedure 430.10(c) is overruled. Such a plea in abatement requires an "absolute identity of parties [and] causes of action." (Plant Insulation Co. v. Fibreboard Corp. (1990) 224 Cal.App.3d 781, 788; Childs v. Eltinge (1973) 29 Cal.App.3d 843, 848 ["a demurrer may be sustained on the ground that another cause of action is pending only when the parties in the two cases are identical and the cause of action and issues in the two actions are substantially the same."].) Here, Menzies is one of two plaintiffs in the declaratory relief action (No. CGC-19-574401), together with its insurer Global Aerospace Underwriting Managers, which is not named as a party in the instant tort action. Moreover, Menzies concedes in its reply that "the declaratory judgment action and the underlying litigation raise separate questions." (Opposition, at 3.) The motion based upon exclusive concurrent jurisdiction is denied. The rule of exclusive concurrent jurisdiction does not apply because the actions are pending in the same court in San Francisco. "We held in Kromm v. Kromm, 84 Cal.App.2d 523 [191 P.2d 115], that there is no priority of jurisdiction between cases filed in the same court in the same county, nor is there any conflict of jurisdiction between them." (Daly v. White (1950) 100 Cal.App.2d 22, 25.) The propriety of staying the declaratory relief action pursuant to Montrose Chemical Corp. v. Superior Court (1993) 6 Cal.4th 287 is not presently before the court.

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: Sean Kane, Not Reported. (302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-19-2019 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:

Nathaniel Lipanovich, Esq. of Thoits Law, T: 650.327.4200, for defendant & moving party, by Court Call;
Richard R. Frischer, Esq. of Lamb & Fischer Law Firm, T: 415.997.9529, for the plaintiff & opposing party.

RULING: The matter was argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT JAVELIN LOGISTICS COMPANY, INC.'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT, as follows:

Defendant Javelin Logistics Company, Inc.'s demurrer to the first amended complaint is overruled.

The demurrer is overruled as to causes of action one through three as plaintiff alleges sufficient facts to impose successor liability on movant. Plaintiff alleges facts showing that movant was a mere continuation of the prior entity. (First Amended Complaint, Paras. 31-34; McClellan v. Northridge Park Townhome Owners Ass'n, Inc. (2001) 89 Cal.App.4th 746, 753 [successor corporation can be liable where "the transaction amounts to a consolidation or merger of the two corporations" or "the purchasing corporation is merely a continuation of the selling corporation."].)

The demurrer is overruled as to cause of action seven. Even if a fraudulent transfer cause of action generally has a heightened pleading standard because it implicates "fraud," that standard is relaxed where "the defendant may be assumed to have knowledge of the facts equal to that possessed by the plaintiff." (Ludgate Ins. Co., Ltd. v. Lockheed Martin Corp. (2000) 82 Cal.App.4th 592, 608; see also Daniels v. Select Portfolio Servicing, Inc. (2016) 246 Cal.App.4th 1150, 1167.) In this case, movant is in the best positon to know the details of the asset purchase agreement and plaintiff alleges sufficient ultimate facts to support the claim. Plaintiff alleges that the transaction was not supported by adequate consideration and the court takes that allegation as true on demurrer. (First Amended Complaint, Paras. 39, 93.)

Movant's authorities are distinguishable. (See Lo v. Lee (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1065, 1072-1075 [order sustaining demurrer affirmed where tuition payments made to a university by an insolvent debtor parent cannot be recovered from the debtor's child, who was not the beneficiary of the transfer]; Fox v. MGM Grand Hotels, Inc. (1982) 137 Cal.App.3d 524, 527 [trial court properly sustained demurrer to fraudulent transfer claim where allegations in the complaint disclosed the transfer of consideration]; Kuhlman v. Pac. States Sav. & Loan Co. (1941) 17 Cal.2d 820, 821-822 [affirming order sustaining demurrer to fraudulent transfer claim where there was no allegation that the consideration was inadequate].)

Order signed in open court. Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com
